design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate alternating horizontal and vertical scrolling stages, collect power-up capsules to enhance weapons, destroy enemies and bosses to progress
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (power-ups) vs. enemy density; maintaining offensive capability while avoiding incoming fire
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Piloting an advanced fighter (Vic Viper or Lord British) through a hostile organism's interior, becoming progressively more lethal through upgrades
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Spin-off that deliberately simplified Gradius' selection-bar system into instant capsule pickups; cooperative two-player design with asymmetric ships; alternating stage orientations break monotony; organic theming (North American version recontextualizes stages as body zones)

difficulty.lives_system: Starts with 3 lives; Konami Code (Up, Up, Down, Down, B, A) extends to 30 lives
difficulty.continues_system: No continues in single-player; two continues available in two-player cooperative mode (configurable via DIP switches)
difficulty.checkpointing: Players continue from death point rather than returning to predefined checkpoints, distinguishing it from standard Gradius behavior
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High via Konami Code accessibility; DIP switch configuration allows arcade operators and ROM hackers to modify continue count

progression.upgrade_system: Power-up capsule system: Speed-Up (Hyper Speed), Missile (Destruct Missile), Ripple Laser (Pulse Laser), Force Field (Shield), Options/Multiples. Capsules must be collected in real-time; options are the only power-ups that persist across ship destruction
progression.unlock_mechanism: Six stages unlock sequentially; stages alternate between horizontal and vertical scrolling to vary gameplay pacing
replayability_hooks: Two-player cooperative mode with asymmetric ship controls (Vic Viper vs. Lord British)
Japanese version features multiple endings tied to continue usage (pilot helmet, Vic Viper, or pilot displayed on staff roll)
Konami Code extends lives dramatically, enabling mastery attempts
Different regional versions (Salamander arcade vs. Life Force arcade vs. NES port) have distinct visual themes and stage backgrounds

world_building_techniques: North American arcade version: opening text establishes alien lifeform setting; narration precedes each stage ('Enter stomach muscle zone', 'Bio-mechanical brain attack')
Japanese Life Force arcade re-release: completely redrawn organic backgrounds and mechanical enemies with biological aesthetics
Environmental storytelling through visual theming: starfield backgrounds (Japanese arcade) vs. web/biological textures (North American version)
Ending cinematic: ship escapes planet as it explodes; Japanese Salamander staff roll includes performance-dependent imagery (pilot/ship variants)

boss_design.philosophy: Pattern-based encounters that reward memorization; bosses emerge at stage endpoints with distinct visual presence; some bosses are preceded by environmental cues (lighting changes, wall collapse)
notable_bosses: Stage 1 Golem: referenced in source data as having unused/repurposed sprite assets
Tetran boss: Japanese Salamander version includes 'blop!' sound effect before fight (removed in Life Force NES)
Stage 5 boss: timing of power meter disappearance differs between Salamander (when boss lights up) and Life Force (when wall starts to collapse), suggesting environmental integration

faction_indicators: Cooperative gameplay emphasizes mutual survival: both Vic Viper and Lord British must navigate stages together
Asymmetric ship selection creates role differentiation between players
danger_signals: High difficulty and pattern memorization create skill-based gatekeeping
Continues system (especially in single-player with zero continues) punishes repeated failure
Power-up capsule dependency creates moments of vulnerability when unpowered
environmental_storytelling: Alien organism interior aesthetics (biological backgrounds, web textures, body-zone naming in North America) establish hostile living ecosystem
Stage transitions between horizontal and vertical scrolling imply navigation through distinct anatomical structures
Explosions and environmental collapse (Stage 5) suggest organism's active resistance to invasion

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor - Life Force NES article)
